title: Improving Language Model Pretraining with Text Structure Information

structured.summary: This paper investigates the impact of surface-level text complexity on transformer-based causal language model pretraining and downstream performance by creating a parallel simplified/human-written English corpus. Models of various sizes are pretrained on each corpus and evaluated on fine-tuning and zero-shot tasks, revealing that smaller models benefit from simpler texts in language modeling perplexity, fine-tuning performance is largely unaffected by complexity, and zero-shot results vary by task type and complexity. The study provides insights on tailoring data curation to downstream objectives and releases resources for further research.

strengths.point: Controlled manipulation of text complexity within a single corpus
strengths.evidence: The authors create parallel simplified and human-written corpora from the same source text, preserving core semantic content while varying surface complexity, enabling a rigorous isolation of complexity effects.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation across multiple model sizes and downstream tasks
strengths.evidence: The study pretrains models ranging from 28M to 500M parameters and evaluates on fine-tuning (GLUE/SuperGLUE) and zero-shot benchmarks covering linguistic knowledge, world knowledge, and commonsense reasoning.

strengths.point: Insightful findings on the interaction between model size, text complexity, and downstream performance
strengths.evidence: Smaller models benefit from simplified text in perplexity, fine-tuning accuracy is negligibly affected by complexity, and zero-shot task performance varies with complexity and task type, guiding data curation strategies.

strengths.point: Release of parallel corpora and pretrained models
strengths.evidence: The paper provides resources to the community to facilitate further investigation into text complexity effects in language model pretraining.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of hallucinations and semantic fidelity in LLM-based simplification
weaknesses.evidence: The paper reports average semantic similarity and ROUGE scores but lacks detailed error annotation or factual consistency metrics to ensure the simplified corpus truly preserves meaning.

weaknesses.point: Limited pretraining regimen with only one epoch and no training curves
weaknesses.evidence: Models are pretrained for a single epoch, which may confound model size and complexity interaction results, especially given unexpected perplexity degradation in largest models.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation omits generative and open-ended tasks critical for causal LLMs
weaknesses.evidence: Assessments focus on classification and zero-shot benchmarks without including generation tasks such as summarization or story generation, limiting understanding of complexity impact on generation quality.

weaknesses.point: Lack of statistical significance testing and effect size reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Performance differences, especially in fine-tuning and zero-shot results, are small and no statistical tests or confidence intervals are provided to substantiate claims.
Prompt templates, LLaMA decoding parameters, full training hyperparameters, and random seeds are not disclosed, complicating replication efforts.

weaknesses.point: Narrow scope limited to causal transformer models and a single domain/language
weaknesses.evidence: Findings may not generalize to encoder or encoder-decoder architectures or to other domains and languages beyond the curated English educational corpus.

questions.question: How does the LLM-based simplification affect factual correctness and hallucination rates in the simplified corpus?
questions.motivation: Understanding semantic fidelity is crucial to trust that observed effects arise from complexity changes rather than introduced errors.

questions.question: Would longer pretraining with multiple epochs alter the interaction between model size and text complexity observed here?
questions.motivation: One epoch may not suffice to reveal stable learning dynamics, especially for larger models.

questions.question: How does text complexity influence open-ended generation quality and diversity in causal LLMs?
questions.motivation: Generative tasks are a core use case for causal LLMs, and complexity may affect fluency, coherence, and factuality.

questions.question: Can the relative contributions of sentence length, lexical diversity, and syntactic complexity to model performance be disentangled?
questions.motivation: Identifying which aspects of complexity matter most could guide targeted data curation or synthetic data generation.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient analysis of hallucinations and semantic fidelity in LLM-based simplification. The paper reports average semantic similarity and ROUGE scores but lacks detailed error annotation or factual consistency metrics to ensure the simplified corpus truly preserves meaning
critique_points.point_002: Limited pretraining regimen with only one epoch and no training curves. Models are pretrained for a single epoch, which may confound model size and complexity interaction results, especially given unexpected perplexity degradation in largest models
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation omits generative and open-ended tasks critical for causal LLMs. Assessments focus on classification and zero-shot benchmarks without including generation tasks such as summarization or story generation, limiting understanding of complexity impact on generation quality
critique_points.point_004: Lack of statistical significance testing and effect size reporting. Performance differences, especially in fine-tuning and zero-shot results, are small and no statistical tests or confidence intervals are provided to substantiate claims
critique_points.point_005: Sparse methodological details hinder reproducibility. Prompt templates, LLaMA decoding parameters, full training hyperparameters, and random seeds are not disclosed, complicating replication efforts
critique_points.point_006: Narrow scope limited to causal transformer models and a single domain/language. Findings may not generalize to encoder or encoder-decoder architectures or to other domains and languages beyond the curated English educational corpus
